verses.commentary: To the disciples: a rich man had a manager (oikonomos, οἰκονόμος). Charges brought against him for wasting (diaskorpizon, διασκορπίζων — same word as the prodigal in 15:13) his master's property.
Master: 'Give an account (logon, λόγον) of your management — you can no longer be manager.' Termination announced; reckoning required first.
Manager to himself: 'What shall I do? My master is taking my position from me. I am not strong enough to dig; I am ashamed to beg.' Inability and shame both named.
'I know what I will do, so that when I am removed from management, people may receive me into their houses.' Strategy formed: create future hospitality through present financial action.
Calls in his master's debtors one by one. Asks the first: 'How much do you owe?'

'A hundred measures of oil (about 875 gallons).' 'Take your bill, sit down quickly, write fifty.' Reducing his commission, or possibly reducing the principal — debated. Either way, the manager builds goodwill at his master's expense.
Another: 'A hundred measures of wheat (about 1100 bushels).' 'Take your bill, write eighty.' Same pattern.

Master commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness (phronimos, φρονίμως). 'The sons of this age are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than the sons of light.' Worldly people apply themselves to their goals more vigorously than spiritual people apply themselves to theirs.

'Make friends for yourselves by means of unrighteous mammon (mamona tes adikias, μαμωνᾶ τῆς ἀδικίας) — that when it fails, they may receive you into eternal dwellings.' Use temporary wealth for eternal relationships. The shrewd manager's strategy redirected to kingdom ends.
One who is faithful in very little is also faithful in much; dishonest in very little is dishonest in much. Scale-invariant character.
If you have not been faithful in unrighteous mammon, who will entrust true riches to you? Money-stewardship as test for greater stewardship.
If not faithful in another's, who will give you what is your own? Stewardship of borrowed (everything is God's) before ownership.
No servant can serve two masters — hate one, love the other; devoted to one, despise the other. You cannot serve God and mammon. Echo of Matthew 6:24.
Pharisees, lovers of money (philargyroi, φιλάργυροι), heard all this — ridiculed (exemyktrizon, ἐξεμυκτήριζον — turned up nose at) him. The teaching cuts.
'You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts. What is exalted among men is an abomination before God.' Reversal of value scales.
'The Law and the Prophets were until John. Since then the good news of the kingdom of God is preached, and everyone forces his way into it.' Salvation history pivot at John.
Easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one tittle (keraia, κεραία — smallest stroke) of the Law to fail. The Law endures.
Divorcing wife and marrying another = adultery. Marrying divorced woman = adultery. Tight standard inserted suddenly — both indicting Pharisees' loose divorce practice.
A rich man dressed in purple (royal cloth) and fine linen (luxury), feasting sumptuously every day. Anonymous — Western tradition names him 'Dives' (Latin for 'rich').
A poor man named Lazarus (Eleazar — God helps) laid at his gate, full of sores. The named poor man — only character named in any Lukan parable.
Desiring to be fed with what fell from the rich man's table. Dogs came and licked his sores. Animals show more compassion than the rich man's household.
Poor man died — carried by angels to Abraham's side (kolpon Abraam, κόλπον Ἀβραάμ — bosom). Rich man also died and was buried. Two deaths, two destinations.
In Hades, in torment, lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far away, Lazarus by his side. Vision across the chasm.
Calls: 'Father Abraham, have mercy — send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, I am in anguish in this flame.' Treats Lazarus as available servant even now.

Abraham: 'Child, remember that you in your lifetime received your good things, and Lazarus in like manner bad. Now he is comforted and you are in anguish.' Reversal of fortune. Justice deferred but realized.
'Besides this — a great chasm (chasma mega, χάσμα μέγα) has been fixed; those who would pass from here to you cannot, neither can any cross from there to us.' Final divide. No mobility post-death.
'I beg you, father — send him to my father's house —'
'for I have five brothers — that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment.' Eschatological evangelism requested.
Abraham: 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.' Scripture is sufficient.
'No, father Abraham — if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent.' Spectacle over scripture.

'If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone should rise from the dead.' Prophetic irony — Jesus' own resurrection will not change hearts already set against scripture. Heart-orientation determines reception of evidence.
